title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an architect who designs public spaces with a focus on how people actually behave in cities. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: Designing for People
content.subtitle: A magazine article about an architect who studies human behaviour to create better urban spaces.
content.text: Most architectural offices have models, drawings and computer renderings on their walls. The office of Nadia Kowalski, in a converted post office building in Vienna, has something else: photographs. Thousands of them, pinned to every surface, documenting the same kinds of scenes over and over — people sitting on steps, leaning against walls, pausing at street corners, eating lunch on benches, watching other people. [31] Kowalski calls these images her 'evidence base', and she insists, with the conviction of someone who has had to convince many sceptical clients, that they are as important to the design process as any technical drawing. [/31] || Her approach places her in a tradition of thinking about cities that dates at least to the urban theorist William H. Whyte, who spent years in New York in the 1970s filming pedestrians in public spaces and using the footage to understand what made some places lively and others deserted. Whyte discovered that the answers were often surprisingly small: whether a ledge was the right height to sit on, whether the sun reached a particular corner in the afternoon, whether there was something to watch. [32] What made his findings radical was not the scale of the interventions they suggested, but the implication that conventional architects and city planners had been paying attention to the wrong things. [/32] || Kowalski has spent twenty years extending Whyte's insights into contemporary European urban contexts. Her current project involves the redesign of a large public square in Bratislava that had been, by common agreement, a failure — a formal, windswept open space that no one chose to spend time in, despite its central location and architectural ambition. Her analysis of the square revealed a familiar pattern. [33] 'It was designed to be looked at,' she told me, 'not to be used. The architects thought about what it would look like from above, or from across the street, but not about what it would feel like to actually be in it for ten minutes.' [/33] || The redesign she proposed was modest in scale but significant in its implications. Rather than demolishing the existing structures, she suggested introducing a series of changes that could be tested and adjusted: movable seating, a small food kiosk, areas of shade, a children's water feature. [34] Several of her clients, she says, are initially disappointed by this kind of proposal — they come expecting bold gestures and get what looks like furniture. [/34] But she is patient with this response. In her experience, the proof comes quickly: people start sitting down, then talking, then returning. || Kowalski is deeply critical of what she calls 'trophy architecture' — the tendency in contemporary city development to commission visually spectacular buildings that perform well in photographs and poorly in daily use. [35] 'A building that appears on every architecture magazine cover but empties its surrounding streets is not a success,' she says. 'It's a very expensive way of making a place worse.' [/35] || When I ask what she considers the most important quality in a well-designed urban space, she answers without hesitation. [36] 'The sense that it was made for you,' she says. 'That whoever designed it was thinking about how you would actually live in it — not how it would look in their portfolio.' [/36]

questions.question: The photographs covering Kowalski's office walls are described in order to show that
options: A) she has an unusual personal interest in street photography.
B) observational evidence of human behaviour is central to her design work.
C) her office is more creative and informal than those of other architects.
D) she prefers to work from photographic references rather than technical drawings.
questions.explanation: Kowalski calls the images her 'evidence base' and insists they are 'as important to the design process as any technical drawing' — making them a core professional tool, not a personal interest.

questions.question: The writer says Whyte's findings were 'radical' because
options: A) he showed that small physical changes could transform how people used public spaces.
B) they challenged the fundamental priorities of traditional urban design.
C) they were the first evidence that architects were creating unpopular public spaces.
D) he was the first researcher to use film as a method of studying pedestrian behaviour.
questions.explanation: The writer says the radicalism lay not in the scale of the interventions but in 'the implication that conventional architects and city planners had been paying attention to the wrong things' — challenging professional priorities.

questions.question: Kowalski's description of the Bratislava square as being 'designed to be looked at' suggests that
options: A) the architects were primarily concerned with the square's visual appearance.
B) tourists were the intended users of the space rather than local residents.
C) the square had been built for official ceremonies rather than everyday use.
D) the design had been selected by a public vote based on architectural drawings.
questions.explanation: Kowalski contrasts 'looked at' with actually 'used' or 'felt', and says the architects focused on appearance 'from above or from across the street' rather than the experience of being in the space — a visual rather than functional priority.

questions.question: Clients who are initially disappointed by Kowalski's proposals are expecting
options: A) proposals that will require a larger financial investment than she is suggesting.
B) more dramatic architectural statements rather than practical small-scale changes.
C) detailed explanations of the research behind her design decisions.
D) designs that reflect the latest trends in contemporary European architecture.
questions.explanation: The writer says clients 'come expecting bold gestures and get what looks like furniture' — the disappointment is because they want dramatic architectural statements and get modest, practical solutions instead.

questions.question: Kowalski's description of 'trophy architecture' as 'a very expensive way of making a place worse' means that
options: A) visually impressive buildings often attract criticism as well as praise.
B) spectacular buildings damage their surroundings despite their visual appeal.
C) the construction costs of iconic buildings are rarely justified by their benefits.
D) famous architecture buildings eventually become outdated and begin to deteriorate.
questions.explanation: Kowalski says a building that 'empties its surrounding streets is not a success' — it actively damages the area around it while being expensive to build, making the place worse despite looking impressive.

questions.question: Kowalski's final answer emphasises that the most important quality of good urban design is
options: A) the ability to attract international attention and architectural recognition.
B) the use of innovative materials and construction techniques.
C) a genuine understanding of and concern for the people who will use the space.
D) a balance between aesthetic ambition and practical construction requirements.
questions.explanation: Kowalski says the key quality is the feeling 'that whoever designed it was thinking about how you would actually live in it' — putting the user's real experience at the centre of the design process.
